channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: As Trump Scraps With Meloni, His Envoy to Italy Is at Sea
item.description: Tilman J. Fertitta, the U.S. ambassador to Rome, is summering on his superyacht as President Trump squabbles with Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni of Italy.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich and Gianni Cipriano
item.media:description: Tilman Fertitta aboard his yacht, Boardwalk, on the Fourth of July.

item.title: They Called Sam Neill ‘Skux.’ (It Was a Compliment.)
item.description: The New Zealand slang word, dating back to the 1990s, was pulled back into circulation in tributes to the actor, who died on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:description: Sam Neill at the Toronto International Film Festival in 2019.

item.title: Iran War Updates: Bridges and Water Plants Hit as Strikes Stretch to 7th Straight Day
item.description: Overnight and into Friday, Iran reported U.S. strikes on infrastructure that can serve civilian purposes and has targeted similar sites in Gulf countries that host American bases. In the afternoon, the U.S. military announced a new round of attacks on Iran.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tehran in early July.

item.title: Burnham Becomes Labour Leader and Britain’s Incoming Prime Minister
item.description: At a time of political upheaval and economic stagnation, Andy Burnham will on Monday become the seventh prime minister in a decade.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Andy Burnham, the new leader of the Labour Party, speaking in London on Friday.

item.title: The 20-Somethings Running Ukraine’s War Machine
item.description: A new generation has been cutting red tape to speed weapons to the front. But the old guard pushed back, and now Ukraine’s young defense minister is out.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut
item.media:credit: Jędrzej Nowicki for The New York Times
item.media:description: A friend’s call for help led Mykhailo Rudominski into defense work. His company, Himera, makes electronic warfare-resistant radios.

item.title: ‘Suddenly, I Was Free’: A Chinese Pastor’s Journey From Jail to the U.S.
item.description: In his first interview since being released by China, Pastor Ezra Jin Mingri details his surprise release after a direct appeal by President Trump to Xi Jinping.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Wang
item.media:description: Pastor Ezra Jin Mingri at his home near Washington on Thursday. His release from jail was a rare concession by China after President Trump raised his case.

item.title: Israeli Strike in Gaza Kills Seven People, Health Officials Say
item.description: The Israeli military said the attack targeted members of the second-biggest militant group in Gaza. It also said it was “aware of the claims that several uninvolved individuals” had been harmed.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon and Abu Bakr Bashir
item.media:description: Palestinians carrying the body of a man killed during an Israeli strike in central Gaza on Friday.

item.title: Prominent Cuban Artist Will Go Into Exile After 5-Year Prison Term
item.description: The United States offered a visa to Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara, who was expected to fly to Miami on Saturday.
item.dc:creator: David C. Adams
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara in Havana in 2021.

item.title: How Andy Burnham, ‘King of the North,’ Conquered U.K. Politics
item.description: A plain-spoken politician from a modest background in northwest England, Mr. Burnham became Labour leader — and Britain’s incoming prime minister — on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Andy Burnham in Ashton-in-Makerfield, England, in June. Mr. Burnham is on the verge of becoming prime minister.

item.title: Beijing Rejects Trump’s Claim of Meddling, but Avoids a Fight
item.description: Chinese officials called the election-meddling accusation baseless, while analysts said Beijing was trying to preserve a fragile détente.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson
item.media:description: President Trump addressing the nation from the East Room of the White House on Thursday.

item.title: Venezuela Earthquakes Force a Grim Homecoming for Migrants
item.description: Mass migration, which defines modern Venezuela, has heavily marked the earthquake, too, saving many who had left, but also making their grief harder.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Liliana Figueroa, who had migrated to Brazil, returned to Venezuela after her daughter, Angelina, was killed in the earthquake.

item.title: 7.3-Magnitude Earthquake Off Coast of Mexico and Guatemala Poses Tsunami Threat
item.description: A powerful earthquake near the border of Mexico and Guatemala raised fears of a tsunami and shook buildings across three countries.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:description: People outside the Biblioteca Nacional in San Salvador, El Salvador, following a 7.4 magnitude earthquake on Friday.

item.title: Parents in Uganda Demand Answers After Bus Crash Kills 21 Children
item.description: The driver of the school bus, carrying dozens of students, lost control, officials said, causing the bus to veer off the road into a boulder.
item.dc:creator: Musinguzi Blanshe
item.media:description: An overturned school bus in eastern Uganda, where 21 students and five adults were killed in the crash.

item.title: Hundreds of Rohingya Feared Drowned at Sea
item.description: Members of the persecuted minority group from Myanmar set off in search of a better life weeks ago, but news of their boats sinking only emerged recently. More than 500 are feared dead.
item.dc:creator: Verena Hölzl
item.media:description: A boat carrying a group of Rohingya refugees landed in Indonesia’s Aceh province last year. Each year, thousands of Rohingya refugees attempt to reach Indonesia, Malaysia or Thailand on overcrowded, unseaworthy boats.

item.title: As Blazes Spread Near Paris, Extreme Heat Is Changing How France Fights Fires
item.description: Until recently, France’s forest fires were mostly in the south. This week, fires are raging much farther north. France is having to rethink its firefighting strategy.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter and Dmitry Kostyukov
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times

item.title: Dubai Migrant Worker Layoffs Reveal Costs of Middle East War
item.description: The Persian Gulf city attracted migrant workers with the promise of opportunity. Many now say the war has brought job losses and salary cuts.
item.dc:creator: Anjana Sankar and Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Natalie Naccache for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Satwa area of Dubai in April. Migrant workers in the city have faced job losses or cuts since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.

item.title: 7 Americans Sent to Disputed Kenya Ebola Site After New Trump Travel Ban
item.description: The aid workers were on the frontline in the fight against the outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo and have shown no symptoms, their employer said.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: A satellite image showing the field hospital at Laikipia Air Base in central Kenya, where America aid workers are being quarantined.

item.title: The Rise and Fall of Ukraine’s Drone Warfare Mastermind
item.description: The popularity of drones, and the defense minister who heavily promoted them, helped lead to a clash in which President Volodymyr Zelensky sided with the military brass.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:credit: Jędrzej Nowicki for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mykhailo Fedorov, the former minister of defense for Ukraine, speaking Thursday in Kyiv. Mr. Fedorov’s growing reliance on drones had reportedly irked some members of the military’s leadership.

item.title: Karim Khan’s Accuser Breaks Silence on Alleged Sexual Misconduct at I.C.C.
item.description: Karim Khan, the chief prosecutor at the International Criminal Court, has denied the accusations. He faces a vote next week that will determine whether he keeps his job.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Taub and Jeanna Smialek
item.media:description: Karim Khan, the chief prosecutor of the International Criminal Court, at a U.N. Security Council meeting.

item.title: War Between U.S. and Iran Expands, With Strikes Across the Region
item.description: Videos and reports in Iranian state media showed damage to bridges, railways and other infrastructure. U.S. allies in the region reported retaliatory strikes by Iran.
item.dc:creator: Leily Nikounazar, Shirin Hakim and Lara Jakes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An image taken from social media and verified by The New York Times showing a damaged portion of a bridge in Hormozgan Province, Iran, on Friday.

item.title: Lam Wing-Kee, Hong Kong Bookseller Who Defied Authorities, Dies at 70
item.description: One of five booksellers kidnapped by Chinese officials in 2015, Mr. Lam spoke out after his release and became an international cause célèbre.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.category: Causeway Bay Books (Causeway Bay, Hong Kong, Retailer)
item.media:credit: An Rong Xu for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lam Wing Kee in 2020 at the Causeway Bay Bookstore in Taipei, Taiwan. Taiwan’s ministry of culture saluted the store as “an inspiration and role model for democracy, liberty and human rights across borders.”

item.title: E.U. Proposes Changes to Emissions Trading System
item.description: The European Union is facing a green backlash as emissions cuts become harder and require bigger overhauls to industry and livelihoods
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.category: Carbon Caps and Emissions Trading Programs
item.media:description: Europe, which often holds itself up as a climate leader, has been facing political infighting over some of its policies.

item.title: Trump Faces the Limits of U.S. Firepower and the Lessons of Past Wars
item.description: Like his predecessors, President Trump has struggled to turn battlefield successes into long-term victories.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We’re going to knock out all their power plants,” President Trump told Fox News this week. “We’re going to knock out all their bridges unless they get to the table and negotiate.”

item.title: Cuban Dissident Was Missing A Week After Prison Release
item.description: A Cuban artist awaiting permission from the Trump administration to enter the United States was held in an unknown location in Cuba.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara in 2018.

item.title: Iran-U.S. Talks: How 4 Negotiators Would Approach Diplomacy
item.description: First step: Agree on a goal.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran in June. The likelihood of an easing of tension between the United States and Iran looks distant at the moment, but there are small signals that diplomacy could still be possible.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Shirin Hakim, Pranav Baskar, Lara Jakes and Max Bearak
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Philippines Condemns AI Video Posted by Chinese State Media Depicting It as a Monkey
item.description: It lodged a diplomatic complaint with Beijing over an A.I.-generated video centered on territorial disputes in the South China Sea.
item.dc:creator: Jason Gutierrez
item.media:description: An electronic board showing areas of the disputed South China Sea during a conference in Manila that marked the 10th anniversary of a ruling that invalidated Beijing’s claims in the waters, on Friday.

item.title: Japan Passes New Law Banning Flag Desecration in Nationalist Push
item.description: The new law is part of a campaign by Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, a hard-line conservative, to build a more patriotic and assertive Japan. Critics say it undermines free speech.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández
item.media:description: New students of the National Defense Academy saluting the Japanese flag at a review parade in Yokosuka, Japan, in April.

item.title: A Russian Political Battle Ends With a Visit From Masked Men
item.description: Boris B. Nadezhdin, an outspoken opponent of the war in Ukraine, says he knows why he was detained: “Among our leadership, there is panic and chaos.”
item.dc:creator: Valerie Hopkins and Alina Lobzina
item.media:credit: Nanna Heitmann for The New York Times
item.media:description: Boris B. Nadezhdin at home in June.

item.title: How Andy Burnham Becomes Britain’s New Prime Minister
item.description: Andy Burnham is set to officially become the next prime minister on Monday, but a number of things have to happen before then. Here’s how everything is expected to play out.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: Andy Burnham in London this month.

item.title: Canada’s Wildfires Are Burning Through the Night
item.description: As overnight temperatures climb across Canada, fires that once spread more slowly after dark are burning around the clock, straining fire crews.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai and Amy Graff
item.media:credit: Ian Willms for The New York Times
item.media:description: The observation deck of the CN Tower on Wednesday evening as wildfire smoke enveloped downtown Toronto.

item.title: Gay-Themed Mediterranean Cruise Turned Away From Turkey and Then Egypt
item.description: In both Muslim-majority countries, official hostility to gay people has increased in recent years. But the same cruise had visited before without problems.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.media:description: The Scarlet Lady in Dover, Britain, in 2020.

item.title: Delightful World Cup Stories
item.description: Not all the best moments from the tournament were on the pitch. We revisit some of our favorites ahead of Sunday’s final.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: Ukraine Was on a Roll. Then a Clash Over War Strategy Exploded Into View.
item.description: From an underground parking garage, Ukraine’s newly dismissed defense minister aired the most dramatic, public critique of the military command to emerge during the war.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:credit: Jędrzej Nowicki for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mykhailo Fedorov, the former minister of defense, speaking in Kyiv on Thursday.

item.title: Astronomers Find an Atmosphere on a Nearby Earthlike Planet
item.description: It’s the first potentially habitable world known to host an atmosphere, making it a lead contender in the search for life beyond our solar system.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: An artist’s concept of the exoplanet LHS 1140b, surrounded by a helium-rich atmosphere. Another rocky planet orbits the same cool red dwarf star in the distance.

item.title: Israeli Government Pushes Through Divisive Laws Before Election
item.description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu backed a series of laws undermining legal oversight of the government, benefiting allied media outlets and aiming to shore up ultra-Orthodox political support.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner and Johnatan Reiss
item.media:description: Protesters against a law banning prosecution of ultra-Orthodox men for evading conscription, in Jerusalem on Wednesday.

item.title: Orphanage Fire Kills Eleven in Algeria
item.description: The fire on Thursday morning at an orphanage near the nation’s capital killed 11 people, including children, and injured a dozen, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:description: The police at the entrance to an orphanage in Mohammadia, a suburb of Algiers, after a fatal fire on Thursday. Algeria’s president said children were among the dead.

item.title: German Man Accused of Drugging and Filming the Rape of 14 Women
item.description: The case has drawn comparisons to the Pelicot trial in France, which started a discussion across Europe about sexual assault and consent.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze

item.title: How the NYT Reported on Khamenei’s Funeral in Iran
item.description: Our correspondent talked about the challenges of covering major news while under surveillance and filing his work without reliable internet access.
item.dc:creator: Mike Abrams
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mourners gathered at the Grand Mosalla in Tehran to pay respects to Ayatollah Ali Khamenei during his funeral.

item.title: Why Netflix’s ‘The Polygamist’ Has Viewers Outside South Africa Talking
item.description: The hit Netflix show follows the life of a wealthy married couple in South Africa as they deal with the pressures of infidelity and appearances.
item.dc:creator: Zimasa Matiwane

item.title: Highway Executive Given 12-Year Sentence Over Deadly Bridge Collapse in Italy
item.description: Giovanni Castellucci oversaw a company that managed the Morandi Bridge in Genoa when it fell down in 2018, killing 43 people.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: Giovanni Castellucci at a news briefing in Madrid in 2018. The collapse of the Morandi Bridge in Genoa became a source of national embarrassment, raising alarms about Italy’s aging infrastructure.

item.title: As Argentina Holds Up Falklands Banner on Defeating England, Here’s What to Know About the War
item.description: Argentina and Britain fought a bloody war over the Falkland Islands in 1982. Decades later, the dispute suffuses matches between England and Argentina.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon

item.title: Pakistan, a mediator in the war, struggles to make itself heard.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier
item.media:description: A billboard displays the images of Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, left, and Syed Asim Munir, Pakistan’s army chief, in Islamabad, Pakistan, in June.

item.title: Global Opinion Shifts Toward Favoring China Over the U.S., Poll Finds
item.description: An annual survey from the Pew Research Center found that more countries felt positively about China than America.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: Global opinion of the United States had already been suffering a steady decline, according to past polls, but this year’s survey shows a precipitous drop-off.

item.title: What to Know About Mykhailo Fedorov, Ukraine’s Ousted Defense Minister
item.description: Mr. Fedorov became the youthful face of Ukraine’s successful drone warfare program. The move to replace him has prompted street protests.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd and Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:credit: Jędrzej Nowicki for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mykhailo Fedorov in Kyiv, Ukraine’s capital, on Thursday. His appointment was seen as an indication that President Volodymyr Zelensky saw drones as the best bet for defeating Russia.

item.title: Sam Neill’s Cause of Death Was Pneumonia, Actor’s Agent Confirms
item.description: The beloved New Zealand actor best known for his turn in “Jurassic Park” had “fought and beaten” lymphoma before he fell ill, according to a statement.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim
item.media:description: Sam Neill died on Monday from pneumonia, his agent confirmed on Thursday.

item.title: Ukraine’s Ousted Defense Minister Attacks the Military’s Old Guard
item.description: Mykhailo Fedorov defended his efforts to modernize the Ukrainian armed forces as thousands of people protested his dismissal.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd, Andrew E. Kramer and Oleksandr Chubko
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesting the dismissal of Ukraine’s defense minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, in Kharkiv on Thursday. Some of the slogans translate to “Bring back Fedorov” and “Fedorov’s dismissal is a gift to the enemy.”

item.title: A 59-Year-Old Hunger Striker Joins a Gen Z Protest Movement in India
item.description: Sonam Wangchuk, a longtime activist, has fasted for 19 days, joining forces with a student-led campaign to change India’s education system.
item.dc:creator: Showkat Nanda and Anupreeta Das
item.media:description: Sonam Wangchuk, lying down, gets a medical checkup during his hunger strike in New Delhi on Wednesday.

item.title: Here’s Where the U.S. and Iran Have Launched Attacks as the War Escalates
item.description: The U.S. military has appeared to widen its targets in Iran, while Iran has fired at bases belonging to American allies in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.description: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak, Shirin Hakim and Farnaz Fassihi

item.title: Hong Kong Police Raid Independent Bookstores and Arrest 5 People
item.description: Other booksellers have been detained in recent months as part of a broad national security crackdown.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson
item.media:description: Police officers loading confiscated items from Have A Nice Stay, an independent bookstore in Hong Kong on Wednesday.

item.title: The Race for A.I. Data Centers Arrives in India
item.description: India is developing new data centers to secure a foothold in the global A.I. race. Our South Asia bureau chief, Mujib Mashal, discusses how a new Google campus’s potential use of the region’s already strained energy and water resources is creating anxiety among local residents.
item.dc:creator: Mujib Mashal, Sutton Raphael and June Kim

item.title: India Is Moving Fast to Build A.I. Data Centers. A Coastal City May Pay the Price.
item.description: With India lagging in the technology, officials are embracing giant data centers. But critics say the megaprojects will use up energy and water, without providing long-term jobs.
item.dc:creator: Mujib Mashal and Hari Kumar
item.media:credit: Priyadarshini Ravichandran for The New York Times
item.media:description: The port city of Visakhapatnam in the state of Andhra Pradesh is rapidly transforming into India’s coastal gateway for global tech infrastructure, fueled by massive A.I. data centers.

item.title: The Quest for ‘Technological Sovereignty’ in Europe (and Why It’s So Hard)
item.description: France and Germany want to quit relying on America and China for key technology like artificial intelligence, but they’re having to choose where to do it.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Mark Landler
item.media:description: Sébastien Lecornu, the French prime minister, has called for France to increase its independence from American technology firms, announcing plans to replace Zoom with a French-developed version.

item.title: In Iran war, Trump Risks Another American ‘Forever War’
item.description: President Trump, who promised to “end wars,” not start them, may have fallen into a familiar presidential trap.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times
item.media:description: A judiciary building damaged in the war as seen from the Golestan palace, in central Tehran, Iran, this month.

item.title: Iranian American Woman Held in Iran on Spying Charges Is Released
item.description: The dual Iranian-U.S. citizen, Dena Karari, had her passport seized in December 2024 and had not been able to leave Iran as she faced espionage charges.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: For England, the World Cup Final Remains Elusive
item.description: The nation’s years of pain continue.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:description: England’s loss prolongs a World Cup drought that has been a national pain point.
